critique.summary: The article describes a custom height-mapped raycasting engine and dungeon crawler game developed for the TI-89 graphing calculator. It highlights the technical features of the engine, including textured walls, stair geometry, enemy sprites, and procedural dungeon generation, all running on the calculator's limited hardware.
mainPoints: The project is a height-mapped raycasting engine and dungeon crawler for the TI-89 calculator.
It features textured walls, stair-step rendering, billboard enemy sprites, and procedural level generation.
The engine runs on a 10MHz Motorola 68000 processor with a 160x100 4-shade grayscale LCD.
The FAT Engine, originally developed in 2002, serves as the foundation for this project.
The source code is released under the MIT License, while the original FAT Engine SDK retains its copyright.
Prebuilt binaries and tools for transferring the game to the calculator are provided.
The project includes demos and additional effects like Conway's Game of Life and plasma effects.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with known technical capabilities of the TI-89 calculator and the FAT Engine, a documented raycasting engine from 2002. The repository provides source code, demos, and detailed instructions for building and running the software, which supports the accuracy of the technical descriptions. There is no indication of exaggerated or false claims.
considerations: The article does not mention the performance limitations or user experience challenges of running such a game on the TI-89 hardware.
There is no discussion of how this project compares to other similar projects or modern alternatives.
The licensing status of the original FAT Engine SDK is noted as unclear, which could affect reuse or redistribution.
The article does not provide external sources or independent reviews to corroborate the project's quality or impact.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around raycasting projects on limited hardware, with users sharing their experiences and asking technical questions about performance and compatibility. There is appreciation for the creativity involved in implementing 3D effects on constrained systems.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
I have been working on a ray-caster for the NES [1].

Ray casters are fun because they aren't that computationally expensive, so it can be a fun way to bolt on 3D to a system that really doesn't support it.

[1] https://youtu.be/2wPT_UD6Ptc I'm still learning how to program for the NES so it's super buggy. I was trying to do the thing that Elite does by having a bunch of different lines as sprites and using that to represent vectors with... interesting results.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed personal experience with raycasting on constrained hardware, explaining the computational aspects and challenges, making it a valuable and accurate contribution.

comments.author: mysterydip
comments.text: I love raycasters! This is great, thanks for sharing. I noticed the “fisheye” effect, was the cosine correction too expensive an operation given the hardware limitations?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a relevant technical question about performance optimization related to the fisheye effect, showing understanding of raycasting challenges on limited hardware.

comments.author: alexshendi
comments.text: As you seem to be the author: will this run on a voyage 200? Also: very impressive.
comments.analysis: This comment asks a straightforward compatibility question and offers praise, but lacks technical depth or additional insight.
